reasoning:
On Friday, August 1, 2025, a significantly weaker-than-expected jobs report and new tariffs sparked a major market sell-off (Dow down 500 points, S&P down 1.6%). While 'safe haven' Treasury yields dropped steeply, the High Yield effective yield increased from 6.86% to 6.93%, demonstrating that credit spreads are widening rapidly due to economic anxieties and risk-off sentiment. This dynamic strongly mirrors the early August 2024 shock. 

In the short term (August 10), credit panic and spread widening typically persist and outpace the decline in underlying Treasury yields, making it highly probable that the HY yield will be higher than the August 3 baseline. 

For the medium-term (September 2) and long-term (November 1 and January 30), the situation becomes more balanced. The Federal Reserve is highly likely to implement rate cuts, dragging the risk-free Treasury components of these bonds heavily downward. For HY yields to remain elevated above ~6.93% into late 2025, credit spreads would have to stay blown out (suggesting a deep recession rather than a soft landing). Historical patterns generally show central bank easing eventually pulling aggregate corporate yields lower once initial panics subside. Thus, the probability of the yield remaining above the August baseline declines steadily as the forecast horizon extends, regressing toward 50%.
